stem: Let \( U \) and \( W \) be subspaces of \( \mathbb{R}^{[[n]]} \) with \( \dim U = [[a]] \), \( \dim W = [[b]] \), and \( \dim(U \cap W) = [[c]] \). What is \( \dim(U + W) \)?
solution: By the dimension formula \( \dim(U+W) = \dim U + \dim W - \dim(U\cap W) = [[a]] + [[b]] - [[c]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to subtract the dimension of the intersection.
This subtracts the intersection twice instead of once.
This adds the intersection dimension instead of subtracting it.
This is off by one from the correct dimension.
This is off by one from the correct dimension.
This misapplies the formula by multiplying the dimensions.
This assumes the sum fills the whole ambient space.
This is off by two from the correct dimension.

stem: Let \( U \) and \( W \) be subspaces of \( \mathbb{R}^{[[n]]} \) with \( \dim U = [[a]] \) and \( \dim W = [[b]] \). Suppose \( U + W = \mathbb{R}^{[[n]]} \) (together they span the whole space). What is \( \dim(U \cap W) \)?
solution: Since \( U+W = \mathbb{R}^{[[n]]} \), \( \dim(U+W) = [[n]] \). By the dimension formula, \( \dim(U\cap W) = \dim U + \dim W - \dim(U+W) = [[a]] + [[b]] - [[n]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This has the wrong sign, as if \( \dim U+\dim W \) were subtracted from \( n \) instead of the reverse.
This forgets to subtract \( \dim(U+W)=n \) at all.
This just reports the ambient dimension itself.
This is off by one from the correct dimension.
This is off by one from the correct dimension.
This subtracts the two given dimensions instead of applying the formula.
This subtracts the two given dimensions in the wrong order.

stem: Let \( U \) and \( W \) be subspaces of \( \mathbb{R}^{[[n]]} \) with \( \dim U = [[a]] \) and \( \dim W = [[b]] \), with no further information given. What is the SMALLEST possible value of \( \dim(U \cap W) \)?
solution: Since \( \dim(U+W) \le [[n]] \), the formula \( \dim(U\cap W) = \dim U + \dim W - \dim(U+W) \) gives \( \dim(U\cap W) \ge [[a]]+[[b]]-[[n]] \); combined with \( \dim(U\cap W)\ge 0 \), the smallest possible value is \( \max(0, [[a]]+[[b]]-[[n]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This omits the floor of \( 0 \), which is wrong whenever \( [[a]]+[[b]] < [[n]] \) (a negative dimension is impossible).
This assumes the subspaces can always be arranged to intersect trivially, ignoring the ambient dimension constraint.
This assumes the two subspaces are always independent (never overlapping) and adds their dimensions.
This is off by one from the correct minimum.
This is off by one from the correct minimum.
This has the wrong sign for the bound.
This computes the LARGEST possible intersection dimension, not the smallest.

stem: Let \( U \) and \( W \) be subspaces of \( \mathbb{R}^{[[n]]} \) with \( \dim U = [[a]] \) and \( \dim W = [[b]] \), with no further information given. What is the LARGEST possible value of \( \dim(U \cap W) \)?
solution: Since \( U \cap W \subseteq U \) and \( U \cap W \subseteq W \), \( \dim(U\cap W) \) can be at most \( \dim U \) and at most \( \dim W \); this bound \( \min([[a]],[[b]]) \) is achieved when the smaller subspace is contained in the larger one, giving \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This assumes the two subspaces could overlap in more dimensions than either one has.
This just reports the ambient dimension, which is unrelated to how large the intersection can be.
This computes \( \max([[a]],[[b]]) \) instead of \( \min([[a]],[[b]]) \).
This is off by one from the correct maximum.
This is off by one from the correct maximum.
This computes the SMALLEST possible intersection dimension, not the largest.
This subtracts the two dimensions instead of taking their minimum.

stem: Let \( U \) be the subspace of \( \mathbb{R}^3 \) spanned by \( \mathbf{u}_1 = (1, 0, [[a]]) \) and \( \mathbf{u}_2 = (0, 1, [[b]]) \) (so \( \dim U = 2 \)), and let \( W \) be the subspace spanned by \( \mathbf{w}_1 = ([[p]], [[q]], [[wz1]]) \) and \( \mathbf{w}_2 = ([[r]], [[s]], [[wz2]]) \). What is \( \dim(U + W) \)?
solution: \( \dim(U+W) \) is the rank of the matrix whose rows are \( \mathbf{u}_1,\mathbf{u}_2,\mathbf{w}_1,\mathbf{w}_2 \) (a spanning set for \( U+W \)), which is \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This assumes \( W \) always contributes an entirely new direction beyond \( U \)'s plane, which need not hold.
This assumes \( W \subseteq U \) always, ignoring that \( W \) may add a new direction.
Four vectors cannot span more than \( \mathbb{R}^3 \), so \( \dim(U+W) \) cannot exceed \( 3 \).
This overcounts the spanning set's rank by one.
This undercounts the spanning set's rank by one.
This assumes only one independent direction among all four vectors.
This assumes all four vectors are dependent, which is not generally true.
